flashcards.back: Propositional Logic is a formal system dealing with propositions (atomic statements that are either true or false) and logical connectives (¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔). It uses truth tables to determine the truth value of compound statements based on the truth values of their atomic components.

flashcards.front: What is First-Order Logic (FOL) and how does it differ from PL?
flashcards.back: First-Order Logic extends propositional logic by adding quantifiers (∀, ∃) and predicates that operate on objects, allowing statements about collections of elements. Unlike PL, FOL can express relationships between objects (e.g., ∀x (Human(x) → Mortal(x))) and reason about properties, not just truth values.

flashcards.front: State the key inference rules in propositional logic.
flashcards.back: Modus Ponens: P → Q, P ⊢ Q. Modus Tollens: P → Q, ¬Q ⊢ ¬P. And-Introduction: P, Q ⊢ P ∧ Q. And-Elimination: P ∧ Q ⊢ P (or Q). Or-Introduction: P ⊢ P ∨ Q. Unit Resolution: P ∨ Q, ¬P ⊢ Q.

Semantic entailment (P ⊨ Q) means that whenever all premises in set P are true, the conclusion Q must also be true in every interpretation (model). Formally: P ⊨ Q iff for all assignments where all statements in P are true, Q is also true. This is a declarative (semantic) relationship, distinct from syntactic derivability (⊢).

flashcards.front: List five important logical equivalences in propositional logic.
flashcards.back: 1) De Morgan's: ¬(P ∧ Q) ≡ ¬P ∨ ¬Q. 2) Implication equivalence: P → Q ≡ ¬P ∨ Q. 3) Contraposition: P → Q ≡ ¬Q → ¬P. 4) Distributive: P ∧ (Q ∨ Q) ≡ (P ∧ Q) ∨ (P ∧ R). 5) Idempotent: P ∧ P ≡ P, P ∨ P ≡ P.

flashcards.front: What are Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) and Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF)?
flashcards.back: CNF is a formula in the form (A ∨ B ∨ ...) ∧ (C ∨ D ∨ ...) ∧ ..., where each clause contains only literals connected by OR, and clauses are connected by AND. DNF is the dual: (A ∧ B ∧ ...) ∨ (C ∧ D ∧ ...) ∨ ..., where each term contains only literals connected by AND, and terms are connected by OR. Any propositional formula can be converted to CNF or DNF.

flashcards.front: What is the Resolution inference rule, and how is it used for automated reasoning?
flashcards.back: Resolution is a rule for refutation proofs: (P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ R) ⊢ (Q ∨ R). For proof by resolution, negate the goal, convert all premises to CNF, apply resolution repeatedly to derive the empty clause (contradiction), thus proving the original goal. This single rule is complete for propositional logic and forms the basis of many automated theorem provers.

flashcards.front: Give two practical applications of propositional and first-order logic in AI.
flashcards.back: 1) Knowledge Representation and Reasoning: Expert systems, ontologies, and semantic web technologies (e.g., OWL) use FOL to represent domain knowledge and automate inference. 2) Automated Planning: STRIPS and PDDL planners represent actions and states using logical formulas, applying inference to find valid action sequences. 3) Logic Programming: Prolog uses Horn clauses (a restricted form of FOL) for rule-based reasoning.
